question: Compare the NuScale passive safety approach described in Ch.01 with the specific passive systems detailed in Ch.05.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It lists tables and figures that provide the relevant diagrams and legends, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios and reduce reliance on external power, resulting in a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The NuScale reactor’s containment vessel (CNV) is kept under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, limits oxygen for combustion, and eliminates the need for insulation. Its passive safety features—including the absence of external coolant loops and reliance on passive cooling—reduce accident risks, eliminate large-break LOCA scenarios, and result in a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment, condenses it, and recirculates water to cool the core, with heat ultimately conducted passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, core design, and safety systems, showing that NuScale uses smaller, simpler, and more integrated components compared to conventional PWRs. The comparison is summarized in tables for easy reference.

nuscale_ch01_0025.title: Control Room Habitability System
nuscale_ch01_0025.summary: The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive safety feature designed to protect plant operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases by supplying clean, compressed air to the main control room (MCR). It maintains positive pressure in protected areas and can provide breathable air for at least 72 hours after an incident, even if AC power is unavailable.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references adherence to RG 1.105 for instrument spans and setpoints and compliance with the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch01_0189.title: BTP 6-2, Rev 3: Minimum Containment Pressure Model for PWR ECCS PerformanceAll (B.1 thru B.3)Various
nuscale_ch01_0189.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria related to containment pressure and water chemistry for PWR ECCS performance. It notes that some criteria partially apply, but specific requirements for systems like containment spray or sump do not, as these are not part of the NuScale design. Additionally, certain guidance, such as minimum containment pressure modeling and bypass leakage paths, is not applicable since NuScale’s design does not result in core uncovery during a LOCA and lacks secondary containment.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s passive decay heat removal system, which uses two independent trains, each capable of removing 100% of the decay heat via natural circulation and passive condensers immersed in the reactor pool. In case of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, the system isolates the affected components and passively transfers heat to a large, stainless steel-lined ultimate heat sink pool, ensuring continued cooling and safety without external power.

nuscale_ch01_0188.title: BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.3 Hydrogen Generation from Aluminum Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0188.summary: This section reviews NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria regarding pH control and hydrogen generation from aluminum corrosion in emergency coolant water for pressurized water reactors. While some criteria reference systems not present in the NuScale design (such as containment spray systems or sumps), the intent of the guidelines is still met due to similar water recirculation processes within the NuScale containment vessel. Other criteria, such as those related to minimum containment pressure and dual containment leakage, are deemed not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational events such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard. No exceptions or non-conformances are noted.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0168.title: SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3: Fission Product Control Systems and StructuresII.2 Secondary Containment
nuscale_ch01_0168.summary: The NuScale design does not include secondary containment or fission product clean-up systems, as its passive safety features and submerged containment vessel inherently control fission products. Therefore, regulatory criteria related to secondary containment and certain fission product control systems are not applicable to the NuScale design, and only specific containment parameters are reviewed for conformance.

nuscale_ch01_0167.title: SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3: Fission Product Control Systems and StructuresII.1 Primary Containment
nuscale_ch01_0167.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3 requirements for fission product control, as its passive containment vessel does not include active fission product clean-up or pressure suppression systems, making some guidance inapplicable. The design does not have secondary containment or other credited engineered safety feature (ESF) fission product control systems, so those criteria are also not applicable. Only the primary containment vessel and its isolation features are credited for fission product control in the NuScale plant.

nuscale_ch01_0043.title: Ultimate Heat Sink
nuscale_ch01_0043.summary: The Ultimate Heat Sink section describes a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool that serves as the final heat removal system for the NuScale plant. In normal operations, heat is removed via a cooling system and external heat sink, while in the event of a total loss of AC power, decay heat is passively transferred to the pool, which has sufficient water inventory to ensure continued cooling through natural circulation and boiling.

nuscale_ch01_0023.title: Containment Systems
nuscale_ch01_0023.summary: The NuScale containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces moisture and corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. In the event of an emergency, passive cooling rapidly reduces pressure and temperature, and the plant’s design—with passive safety systems and a small fuel inventory—improves safety and lowers core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0331.title: SRP 9.2.5, Rev 3: Ultimate Heat SinkII.4 Cooling Water System Inspection
nuscale_ch01_0331.summary: The NuScale design fully conforms to SRP 9.2.5, Rev 3, Section II.4 regarding Cooling Water System Inspection, with no exceptions or comments noted. This indicates that the inspection requirements for the ultimate heat sink's cooling water system are met as specified in the regulatory criteria.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The test program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves—including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures, and in borated coolant—using a test setup that replicated the actual valve configuration and included unique features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility to ensure the valves met safety and operational requirements.
